topic: A coach has to cut a player from the team
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the locker room was thick enough to choke on. Liam stood by his duffel bag, his knuckles white where he gripped the worn leather. Coach Miller’s announcement had landed like a physical blow, a sharp, unwelcome jolt that left him breathless. "We've made a difficult decision regarding the roster," the coach had stated, his voice flat and devoid of warmth. Liam felt a strange heat rising in his chest, a prickly sensation that made the fine hairs on his arms stand up. He looked up, meeting the coach's gaze, and for a split second, he saw a flicker of something—maybe pity, maybe just professional detachment—that only amplified the sting. He didn't say anything. He just slowly zipped his bag, the sound of the zipper echoing unnervingly loud in the sudden vacuum of shared space. He turned and walked toward the exit, each step measured, deliberate, as if trying to contain the tremor running through him.

[story 2]

"You're not going to believe this," Marcus muttered to his teammate, his voice tight. He watched Coach Davies shake his head, a slow, deliberate movement that seemed to convey finality. When the coach finally spoke, the words were clipped, efficient, and utterly dismissive of Marcus’s contributions. Marcus felt a sudden, hot flush creep up his neck, staining his cheeks crimson. He clenched his jaw so hard his teeth ached. He didn't want to argue; the sheer unfairness of the situation made his throat constrict. He focused intently on the scuff marks on the polished floor, his gaze fixed there, refusing to acknowledge the coach’s authority or the sting of the rejection. He just stood there, radiating a coiled tension, waiting for the moment he could physically walk away.

[story 3]

"This is just the way it has to be," Coach Reynolds announced, tapping a pen against the clipboard with an air of weary finality. Elias felt a strange, cold numbness spreading through his limbs. He looked at the coach, his expression carefully blank, but the muscles in his neck were rigid, fighting a sudden urge to lash out. He felt a profound sense of being undervalued, as if all the hours he’d poured into the team had simply evaporated. When the coach concluded, Elias simply nodded once, a tiny, sharp movement. He picked up his water bottle, his hand trembling just slightly, and walked toward the door. The sound of his footsteps seemed too loud, a declaration of silent protest against the abrupt end of his season.
